topics.title: Data Structures: Basic Concepts and Definitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST611 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Structures: Basic Concepts and Definitions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Data Structure** is a systematic, mathematical and logical way of organizing, storing, and managing data in computer memory such that it can be accessed and utilized efficiently. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme module descriptor for OECST611, data structures provide the **structural foundation** upon which algorithms operate, defining the **relationship between data items**, the **operations permitted** on them, and the **performance guarantees** (time and space) offered by various storage schemes.

Formally, a data structure is defined as a triplet:
$$
DS = (D, R, O)
$$
where:
- $D$ = Set of data elements (finite, well-defined domain)
- $R$ = Set of relationships/relations binding the data elements
- $O$ = Set of permissible operations on the data

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The KTU syllabus explicitly treats data structures as the *bridge between the abstract problem domain* and its *concrete machine implementation*. Examiners frequently test the distinction between **logical (abstract)** and **physical (storage)** views of a data structure.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Library Intuition

Imagine a large library containing millions of books. Simply piling books on the floor is "data" without structure. A library becomes useful only when we apply **structure** — shelves, catalogues, indexing systems, and section-wise organization.

| Library Element | Data Structure Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Book | Data element / Record |
| Shelf arrangement | Storage scheme (Array, Linked List) |
| Catalogue / Index | Search structure (Hash Table, BST) |
| Librarian's rulebook | Set of operations (insert, delete, search) |
| Time to find a book | Time complexity of an algorithm |

Just as a librarian selects the right organizational method (Dewey Decimal, alphabetical, genre-based) depending on the use case, a programmer selects the appropriate data structure depending on the operations that dominate the application (searching, sorting, inserting, deleting).

## 1.3 Primitive vs Non-Primitive Data Structures

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (Board Exam Favourite):**
> - **Primitive Data Structures** are the basic, atomic data types directly supported by a programming language. They cannot be broken down into simpler data elements.
> - **Non-Primitive Data Structures** are derived/composite structures formed by combining primitive data types, designed to store collections of values and the relationships among them.

**Primitive Types** (language-built-in):
- Integer (`int`)
- Float (`float`, `double`)
- Character (`char`)
- Boolean (`bool`)
- Pointer / Reference

**Non-Primitive Types** (programmer-defined):
- Arrays, Strings, Structures, Unions
- Linear: Stack, Queue, Linked List
- Non-Linear: Trees, Graphs, Heaps
- Hash-based: Hash Tables

## 1.4 Classification of Data Structures — KTU Taxonomy

The KTU 2024 scheme classifies data structures along **two orthogonal axes**:

**Axis 1 — By Organization:**
- **Linear:** Elements arranged in a sequential order (Array, Stack, Queue, Linked List)
- **Non-Linear:** Elements arranged in a hierarchical or networked manner (Trees, Graphs)

**Axis 2 — By Memory Allocation:**
- **Static:** Size fixed at compile time (Arrays)
- **Dynamic:** Size can grow/shrink at run time (Linked Lists, Trees)

A third classification often tested:
- **Homogeneous vs Heterogeneous** (whether all elements share the same type)

## 1.5 Abstract Data Type (ADT) — A Crucial KTU Distinction

An **Abstract Data Type (ADT)** is a mathematical model for data types where a data type is defined by its **behavior (semantics)** from the point of view of a *user*, specifically in terms of possible values, possible operations on data of this type, and the behavior of these operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ADT ≠ Data Structure.**
> - **ADT** is a *theoretical/logical specification* (what operations exist and what they do).
> - **Data Structure** is the *concrete implementation* (how those operations are actually coded in memory).

**Example:** A `Stack` ADT specifies operations `push(x)`, `pop()`, `peek()`, `isEmpty()`. It can be implemented using either an *array* (static) or a *linked list* (dynamic). The ADT remains the same; only the data structure (implementation) changes.

## 1.6 Why Study Data Structures?

Three engineering motivations:
1. **Efficiency:** Right structure → drastically lower time/space cost (e.g., Binary Search = $O(\log n)$ vs Linear Search = $O(n)$).
2. **Abstraction:** ADTs allow modular, maintainable code.
3. **Problem-solving power:** Many complex problems (shortest path, database indexing, AI search) are unsolvable efficiently without appropriate structures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Highlight:** Performance in data structures is **never** measured in absolute seconds. It is measured in terms of **asymptotic complexity** (Big-O, Theta, Omega notation) — independent of hardware.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operations on Data Structures

Regardless of whether a structure is linear or non-linear, the KTU 2024 syllabus identifies **five canonical operations** that any data structure must efficiently support:

1. **Traversal** — Visiting every element exactly once.
2. **Insertion** — Adding a new element at a specified position.
3. **Deletion** — Removing an existing element.
4. **Searching** — Locating an element satisfying a given property.
5. **Sorting** — Arranging elements in a specified order (ascending/descending).

Additional auxiliary operations include **Merging** (combining two structures), **Splitting**, and **Updating** values.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Two-mark questions frequently ask: *"List the basic operations on data structures"* or *"Distinguish between searching and sorting."* Memorize the five operations above and the subtle difference between **searching** (locate) and **sorting** (arrange).

## 2.2 The ADT Specification Pattern

A formally defined ADT in KTU examination answers should follow this template:

| Component | Description | Example (Stack ADT) |
|---|---|---|
| **Data** | Logical description of the data | Linear list of elements $L = \{a_1, a_2, ..., a_n\}$ with $n \geq 0$ |
| **Operations** | Set of allowed operations | `push(x)`, `pop()`, `peek()`, `isEmpty()`, `size()` |
| **Pre-conditions** | Conditions that must hold before operation | `pop()` requires $n > 0$ |
| **Post-conditions** | Conditions guaranteed after operation | After `pop()`, top element is removed and returned |
| **Error conditions** | Failure behaviour | `pop()` on empty stack → *UNDERFLOW* error |

## 2.3 Algorithm Analysis — The Need for Asymptotic Notation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Core:** Performance of an algorithm (and indirectly, a data structure) is measured by **Time Complexity** $T(n)$ and **Space Complexity** $S(n)$, where $n$ is the size of the input.

Why not just measure in seconds?
- Hardware varies (Intel i3 vs Apple M3 differs by 10×)
- Implementation language varies (C vs Python)
- Input size varies (10 elements vs 10 billion)

Therefore, KTU adopts **asymptotic analysis** — study of the **growth rate** of $T(n)$ as $n \to \infty$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Asymptotic Notations

| Notation | Formal Definition (Big-O, Theta, Omega) | Intuitive Meaning | Engineering Analogy |
|---|---|---|---|
| $f(n) = O(g(n))$ | $\exists c>0, n_0>0$ such that $0 \le f(n) \le c \cdot g(n)$ for all $n \ge n_0$ | $f$ grows **no faster** than $g$ (upper bound) | Maximum wait time guarantee |
| $f(n) = \Omega(g(n))$ | $\exists c>0, n_0>0$ such that $0 \le c \cdot g(n) \le f(n)$ for all $n \ge n_0$ | $f$ grows **no slower** than $g$ (lower bound) | Minimum resource demand |
| $f(n) = \Theta(g(n))$ | $\exists c_1, c_2, n_0>0$ such that $c_1 \cdot g(n) \le f(n) \le c_2 \cdot g(n)$ | $f$ grows **at the same rate** as $g$ (tight bound) | Best and worst match |
| $f(n) = o(g(n))$ | $\lim_{n \to \infty} f(n)/g(n) = 0$ | $f$ grows **strictly slower** than $g$ | Insignificant difference |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Markdown Safety Note (rendered safely above):** In your own KTU exam answer sheets, when writing $O(\vert x \vert)$, never write the vertical pipe `|` inside markdown tables — it breaks the table parser. Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ instead.

## 2.5 Growth Rate Hierarchy (Decreasing Order of Cost)

$$
O(n!) \;\gt\; O(2^n) \;\gt\; O(n^2) \;\gt\; O(n \log n) \;\gt\; O(n) \;\gt\; O(\log n) \;\gt\; O(1)
$$

This ordering is **strictly defined**: $O(1) \subset O(\log n) \subset O(n) \subset O(n \log n) \subset O(n^2) \subset O(2^n) \subset O(n!)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** $O(1)$ powers **Hash Tables** (used in DB indexing, caches). $O(\log n)$ powers **Binary Search Trees and Heaps** (used in routing, priority queues). $O(n \log n)$ is the **theoretical lower bound for comparison-based sorting** — the reason Mergesort/Quicksort exist.

## 2.6 Time Complexity Analysis — Counting the Basic Operations

To find $T(n)$ for an algorithm, **count the number of basic operations** (additions, comparisons, assignments) as a function of input size $n$. Example patterns:

| Code Pattern | Cost | Resulting $T(n)$ |
|---|---|---|
| Single statement, no loop | $c$ (constant) | $O(1)$ |
| One simple loop `for(i=0; i<n; i++)` | $n$ iterations | $O(n)$ |
| Nested loop, both size $n$ | $n \times n$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| Loop halving each time `i = i/2` | $\log_2 n$ iterations | $O(\log n)$ |
| Outer loop $n$, inner loop halves | $n \cdot \log n$ | $O(n \log n)$ |
| Two consecutive $n$-loops | $n + n$ | $O(n)$ (dominant term) |
| Recursive Fibonacci (naive) | Two recursive calls per node | $O(2^n)$ |

## 2.7 Best, Worst, and Average Case Analysis

For an algorithm operating on input of size $n$:
- **Best Case** $\Omega$: Minimum operations over all possible inputs of size $n$.
- **Worst Case** $O$: Maximum operations over all possible inputs of size $n$. *(Most important in KTU exams — gives performance guarantee.)*
- **Average Case** $\Theta$: Expected operations assuming uniform distribution of inputs.

**Example:** Linear search for element $x$ in array of size $n$.
- Best case: $x$ at first position $\Rightarrow T(n) = O(1)$
- Worst case: $x$ at last position or absent $\Rightarrow T(n) = O(n)$
- Average case: $x$ equally likely at any position $\Rightarrow T(n) = \Theta(n)$

## 2.8 Properties of Asymptotic Notations (For 7-Mark Derivations)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reflexivity:} \quad & f(n) = O(f(n)) \\
\text{Transitivity:} \quad & f(n) = O(g(n)) \text{ and } g(n) = O(h(n)) \Rightarrow f(n) = O(h(n)) \\
\text{Symmetry:} \quad & f(n) = \Theta(g(n)) \iff g(n) = \Theta(f(n)) \\
\text{Transpose Symmetry:} \quad & f(n) = O(g(n)) \iff g(n) = \Omega(f(n)) \\
\text{Sum Rule:} \quad & \text{If } f_1(n) = O(g_1(n)) \text{ and } f_2(n) = O(g_2(n)), \\
& \text{then } (f_1 + f_2)(n) = O(\max(g_1(n), g_2(n))) \\
\text{Product Rule:} \quad & f_1(n) \cdot f_2(n) = O(g_1(n) \cdot g_2(n))
\end{aligned}
$$

These identities appear in **2-mark short-answer questions** almost every KTU exam cycle.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Time Complexity of a Triple-Nested Loop

**Problem Statement:** Derive the asymptotic time complexity $T(n)$ of the following algorithm fragment in Big-O notation:

```
Algorithm: TripleSum(A, n)
Input:  An array A of n integers
Output: Count of triplets (i, j, k) with i < j < k and A[i] < A[j] < A[k]

1.  count ← 0
2.  for i ← 0 to n-1 do              // Outer loop
3.     for j ← i+1 to n-1 do          // Middle loop
4.        for k ← j+1 to n-1 do       // Inner loop
5.           if A[i] < A[j] and A[j] < A[k] then
6.              count ← count + 1
7.  return count
```

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Count iterations of the innermost loop for fixed (i, j):**
For given $i$ and $j$, the variable $k$ ranges from $j+1$ to $n-1$. The number of values $k$ takes is:
$$
n - 1 - (j + 1) + 1 = n - j - 1
$$

**Step 2 — Count iterations of the middle loop for fixed $i$:**
For fixed $i$, $j$ ranges from $i+1$ to $n-2$ (we need at least $j+1 \le n-1$ for $k$ to exist). The total work done inside the middle loop is:
$$
T_{mid}(i) = \sum_{j=i+1}^{n-2} (n - j - 1)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $m = n - j - 1$.** When $j = i+1$, $m = n - i - 2$. When $j = n-2$, $m = 1$. The summation becomes:
$$
T_{mid}(i) = \sum_{m=1}^{n-i-2} m = \frac{(n-i-2)(n-i-1)}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum over all values of $i$ from 0 to $n-3$:**
$$
T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-3} \frac{(n-i-2)(n-i-1)}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $p = n - i - 2$.** When $i=0$, $p = n-2$. When $i = n-3$, $p = 1$. The summation reverses to:
$$
T(n) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{p=1}^{n-2} p(p+1) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{p=1}^{n-2} (p^2 + p)
$$

**Step 6 — Apply standard summation formulas:**
$$
\sum_{p=1}^{n-2} p = \frac{(n-2)(n-1)}{2}, \qquad \sum_{p=1}^{n-2} p^2 = \frac{(n-2)(n-1)(2n-3)}{6}
$$

Therefore:
$$
T(n) = \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{(n-2)(n-1)(2n-3)}{6} + \frac{(n-2)(n-1)}{2}\right]
$$

**Step 7 — Factor out the common term $\frac{(n-2)(n-1)}{2}$:**
$$
T(n) = \frac{(n-2)(n-1)}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \left[\frac{2n-3}{3} + 1\right] = \frac{(n-2)(n-1)(2n)}{12}
$$

**Step 8 — Expand to find the leading term:**
$$
T(n) = \frac{n(n-1)(n-2)}{6} = \frac{n^3 - 3n^2 + 2n}{6}
$$

**Step 9 — Apply asymptotic dominance (drop lower-order terms and constants):**
$$
T(n) = \frac{n^3}{6} - \frac{3n^2}{6} + \frac{2n}{6} \approx \frac{n^3}{6} \quad \text{as } n \to \infty
$$

**Step 10 — Final asymptotic bound:**
$$
\boxed{T(n) = O(n^3)}
$$

This is the famous **count-of-increasing-triplets** algorithm used in stock-market analytics.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Big-O Proof by Definition

**Problem Statement:** Prove that $f(n) = 5n^3 + 2n^2 + 7$ is $O(n^3)$.

**Proof (by construction of witnesses $c$ and $n_0$):**

**Step 1 — Restate the definition of Big-O.** We must find positive constants $c$ and $n_0$ such that:
$$
0 \le f(n) \le c \cdot n^3 \quad \text{for all } n \ge n_0
$$

**Step 2 — Bound the lower-order terms.** For $n \ge 1$:
$$
2n^2 \le 2n^3 \quad \text{(since } n^2 \le n^3 \text{ when } n \ge 1\text{)}
$$
$$
7 \le 7n^3 \quad \text{(since } 1 \le n^3 \text{ when } n \ge 1\text{)}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute these bounds into $f(n)$:**
$$
f(n) = 5n^3 + 2n^2 + 7 \le 5n^3 + 2n^3 + 7n^3 = 14n^3
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the witnesses:**
$$
c = 14, \quad n_0 = 1
$$

**Step 5 — Verify the inequality holds:** For all $n \ge 1$:
$$
0 \le 5n^3 + 2n^2 + 7 \le 14n^3 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 6 — Conclude:** By the formal definition of Big-O,
$$
\boxed{5n^3 + 2n^2 + 7 = O(n^3)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Tip:** KTU valuation scripts award **full 7 marks only** if you explicitly state the witnesses $c$ and $n_0$, *not* just the final result. A common mistake is writing "it is obvious" — that fetches zero marks.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Data Structure Categories

| Property | Array (Static Linear) | Linked List (Dynamic Linear) | Stack (LIFO) | Queue (FIFO) | Tree (Non-Linear) | Graph (Non-Linear) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Memory** | Contiguous | Scattered (nodes + pointers) | Depends on impl. | Depends on impl. | Hierarchical, scattered | Nodes + edges, scattered |
| **Access Time** | $O(1)$ random | $O(n)$ sequential | $O(n)$ (or $O(1)$ top) | $O(n)$ (or $O(1)$ front) | $O(\log n)$ BST, $O(n)$ worst | $O(V+E)$ traversal |
| **Insertion** | $O(n)$ (shifting) | $O(1)$ at head | $O(1)$ push | $O(1)$ enqueue | $O(\log n)$ BST | $O(1)$ adjacency update |
| **Deletion** | $O(n)$ (shifting) | $O(1)$ given pointer | $O(1)$ pop | $O(1)$ dequeue | $O(\log n)$ BST | $O(V+E)$ |
| **Search** | $O(n)$ linear, $O(\log n)$ binary | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ BST | $O(V+E)$ DFS/BFS |
| **Static/Dynamic** | Static | Dynamic | Both | Both | Dynamic | Dynamic |
| **Wasted Space** | Possible (over-allocation) | Extra pointer overhead | Minimal | Minimal | Pointer overhead | High overhead |
| **Real-world Use** | Matrices, buffers | OS scheduling, undo | Function calls, recursion | CPU scheduling, BFS | File system, DB index | Social networks, maps |

## 3.4 Worked Derivation 3 — Logarithmic Loop Bound

**Problem Statement:** Determine $T(n)$ for:
```
1. i ← 1
2. while i < n do
3.    i ← 2 * i
4. end while
```

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** After $k$ iterations, the value of $i$ is $2^k$ (since it starts at 1 and doubles each step).

**Step 2:** The loop terminates when $i \ge n$, i.e., when $2^k \ge n$, which gives:
$$
k \ge \log_2 n
$$

**Step 3:** The total number of iterations is the smallest integer $k$ satisfying the above:
$$
\boxed{T(n) = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1 = \Theta(\log n)}
$$

**Real-world application:** This is exactly the inner loop of **Binary Search** — the reason search engines and database B-trees achieve $O(\log n)$ lookup.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Overall Classification of Data Structures

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Data Structures]
    rootA --> primA[Primitive Types]
    rootA --> nonprimA[Non-Primitive Types]

    primA --> p1[Int]
    primA --> p2[Float]
    primA --> p3[Char]
    primA --> p4[Bool]
    primA --> p5[Pointer]

    nonprimA --> linA[Linear]
    nonprimA --> nonlinA[Non-Linear]

    linA --> statA[Static]
    linA --> dynA[Dynamic]
    statA --> s1[Arrays]
    dynA --> d1[Linked List]
    dynA --> d2[Stack]
    dynA --> d3[Queue]

    nonlinA --> nl1[Trees]
    nonlinA --> nl2[Graphs]
    nonlinA --> nl3[Heaps]
    nonlinA --> nl4[Hash Tables]

    nl1 --> t1[Binary Tree]
    nl1 --> t2[BST]
    nl1 --> t3[AVL Tree]
    nl1 --> t4[B-Tree]
    nl2 --> g1[Directed]
    nl2 --> g2[Undirected]
    nl2 --> g3[Weighted]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — ADT vs Data Structure Distinction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    problemA[Real World Problem] --> adtStep[Define ADT]
    adtStep --> specStep[Specify Operations and Semantics]
    specStep --> chooseImpl{Choose Implementation}
    chooseImpl --> arrImpl[Array-based Implementation]
    chooseImpl --> llImpl[Linked List Implementation]
    arrImpl --> dsA[Data Structure Concrete]
    llImpl --> dsB[Data Structure Concrete]
    dsA --> appStep[Application Program]
    dsB --> appStep
    appStep --> userA[End User]

    adtStep -.WHAT.-> userA
    chooseImpl -.HOW.-> userA
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Architecture — Algorithm Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    inpA[Input of Size n] --> algoA[Algorithm Execution]
    algoA --> opCount[Count Basic Operations]
    opCount --> funcStep[Express as Function T of n]
    funcStep --> asymStep[Apply Asymptotic Notation]
    asymStep --> finalStep[Final Complexity O Theta Omega]
    finalStep --> compareStep[Compare Growth Rates]
    compareStep --> rankOut[Rank Algorithms by Efficiency]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Memory Layout of Primitive vs Non-Primitive

```mermaid
graph TB
    memA[Main Memory]
    memA --> stackMem[Stack Segment - Static Binding]
    memA --> heapMem[Heap Segment - Dynamic Binding]

    stackMem --> pArr[Primitive Variables]
    pArr --> pa1[Integer x at fixed offset]
    pArr --> pa2[Float y at fixed offset]
    pArr --> pa3[Character c at fixed offset]

    heapMem --> npStruct[Non-Primitive Structures]
    npStruct --> nps1[Array of 100 ints - contiguous 400 bytes]
    npStruct --> nps2[Linked List Nodes - scattered with pointers]
    nps2 --> nps2a[Node 1 at address 0x100]
    nps2 --> nps2b[Node 2 at address 0x4F0]
    nps2 --> nps2c[Node 3 at address 0x220]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram — Time Complexity Growth Curve Comparison

```mermaid
graph TD
    axisA[Input Size n Increases Rightward] --> costA[Computational Cost Increases Upward]
    costA --> g1[O of 1 - Constant Flat Line]
    costA --> g2[O of log n - Very Slow Rise]
    costA --> g3[O of n - Linear Diagonal]
    costA --> g4[O of n log n - Slightly Steeper]
    costA --> g5[O of n squared - Parabolic]
    costA --> g6[O of 2 to the n - Explosive]

    g1 --> algA[Hash Lookup]
    g2 --> algB[Binary Search]
    g3 --> algC[Linear Search]
    g4 --> algD[Mergesort]
    g5 --> algE[Bubble Sort]
    g6 --> algF[Naive TSP Recursion]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define the term *data structure*. List the basic operations performed on data structures.

**Model Answer:**

A *data structure* is a specialized format for organizing, processing, retrieving, and storing data. It defines the way data is stored, the relationships among the data items, and the operations that can be performed on the data so that it can be used efficiently.

The basic operations performed on data structures are:
1. **Traversal** — Accessing each element exactly once.
2. **Insertion** — Adding a new element to the structure.
3. **Deletion** — Removing an existing element.
4. **Searching** — Finding the location of a particular element.
5. **Sorting** — Arranging the elements in a particular order (ascending/descending).

*— 3 marks*

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Distinguish between a *Primitive* data structure and a *Non-Primitive* data structure with two examples each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Primitive | Non-Primitive |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Basic data types directly supported by the language | Derived structures formed by combining primitive types |
| Size | Fixed (e.g., 4 bytes for `int`) | Variable, depends on number of elements |
| Examples | `int`, `float`, `char`, `bool` | Array, Stack, Queue, Tree, Graph |
| Atomicity | Cannot be decomposed further | Can be decomposed into primitives |
| Storage | Direct memory allocation | Needs additional metadata (pointers, length) |

*— 3 marks*

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Full Solution

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **(a)** Explain the concept of an Abstract Data Type (ADT). Discuss how an ADT differs from a data structure using the *Stack* as an illustrative example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the three asymptotic notations: Big-O, Big-Theta, and Big-Omega, with mathematical expressions and a graph sketch. Give one example algorithm for each notation type. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution (a) — ADT and Stack (7 marks)

**Definition:** An Abstract Data Type (ADT) is a theoretical concept in computer science that defines a data type purely by its behavior (semantics) from the point of view of a user. It specifies **what** operations are performed and **what** the results are, while hiding the **how** (implementation details).

**Key Components of an ADT specification:**
1. **Data definition** — Logical description of the data elements and their relationships.
2. **Operations** — Set of valid operations.
3. **Pre-conditions and post-conditions** — Logical constraints.
4. **Error conditions** — Behaviour under invalid inputs.

**Difference between ADT and Data Structure:**

| ADT | Data Structure |
|---|---|
| Logical / abstract specification | Concrete / physical implementation |
| Defines **what** operations do | Defines **how** operations are implemented |
| Implementation-independent | Implementation-specific (array vs linked list) |
| Concerned with interface | Concerned with storage and algorithms |

**Stack ADT — Illustration:**

*Data:* Linear list $S = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n\}$, top at position $n$, $n \ge 0$.

*Operations:*
- `push(x)` — Insert $x$ at top; pre-condition: stack is not full (in fixed-size impl.); post-condition: $n \leftarrow n+1$.
- `pop()` — Remove and return top element; pre-condition: $n > 0$; on $n=0$ → *UNDERFLOW*.
- `peek()` — Return top element without removal.
- `isEmpty()` — Return true if $n = 0$.
- `size()` — Return $n$.

**Implementation Variability:** The same Stack ADT can be realized using:
- An **array** of fixed size $N$ → static data structure.
- A **linked list** with `head` pointer → dynamic data structure.

In both cases, the *user* sees identical behavior. This proves that **ADT is independent of data structure implementation**.

*— 7 marks*

#### Solution (b) — Asymptotic Notations (7 marks)

**Big-O (Upper Bound):** $f(n) = O(g(n))$ if there exist positive constants $c$ and $n_0$ such that:
$$
0 \le f(n) \le c \cdot g(n) \quad \text{for all } n \ge n_0
$$
*Example:* Linear search has worst-case time $T(n) = 3n + 5 = O(n)$.

**Big-Omega (Lower Bound):** $f(n) = \Omega(g(n))$ if there exist positive constants $c$ and $n_0$ such that:
$$
0 \le c \cdot g(n) \le f(n) \quad \text{for all } n \ge n_0
$$
*Example:* Linear search has best-case time $T(n) = 4 = \Omega(1)$.

**Big-Theta (Tight Bound):** $f(n) = \Theta(g(n))$ if there exist positive constants $c_1, c_2$ and $n_0$ such that:
$$
c_1 \cdot g(n) \le f(n) \le c_2 \cdot g(n) \quad \text{for all } n \ge n_0
$$
*Example:* Mergesort has $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$ for all $n$.

**Graphical Representation:**

For Big-O: $f(n)$ lies **below or on** $c \cdot g(n)$ for $n \ge n_0$.
For Big-$\Omega$: $f(n)$ lies **above or on** $c \cdot g(n)$ for $n \ge n_0$.
For Big-$\Theta$: $f(n)$ lies **sandwiched** between $c_1 \cdot g(n)$ and $c_2 \cdot g(n)$ for $n \ge n_0$.

*— 7 marks*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** **(a)** Classify data structures in detail. Give a comparison chart of linear versus non-linear data structures. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following algorithm. Derive its time complexity $T(n)$ in Big-O notation and identify the best, worst, and average cases. **(7 marks)**

```
Algorithm: MysterySearch(A, n, key)
1.  for i ← 0 to n-1 do
2.     if A[i] = key then
3.        return i
4.  return -1
```

#### Solution (a) — Classification (7 marks)

Data structures are classified along **two primary axes**:

**1. By Organization:**
- **Linear Data Structures:** Elements form a sequence/linear order. Each element has a unique predecessor and successor (except first and last). Examples: Array, Linked List, Stack, Queue.
- **Non-Linear Data Structures:** Elements exhibit hierarchical or networked relationships. Examples: Trees (binary, BST, AVL, B-tree), Graphs (directed, undirected, weighted), Heaps.

**2. By Memory Allocation:**
- **Static:** Size determined at compile time, e.g., Arrays. Faster access, but inflexible.
- **Dynamic:** Size grows/shrinks at run time, e.g., Linked Lists, Trees. Memory-efficient, slightly slower access.

**Comparison Chart — Linear vs Non-Linear:**

| Property | Linear | Non-Linear |
|---|---|---|
| Arrangement | Sequential, one level | Hierarchical or networked |
| Traversal | Single pass, linear order | Multiple levels, recursive |
| Implementation complexity | Simple | Complex |
| Memory utilization | May waste space (arrays) | Usually efficient (linked) |
| Examples | Array, Stack, Queue, Linked List | Tree, Graph, Heap |
| Time complexity typical | $O(n)$ traversal | $O(\log n)$ BST, $O(V+E)$ graph |
| Real-world use | Queues in OS, stacks in compilers | File systems, social networks, DBs |

*— 7 marks*

#### Solution (b) — Time Complexity of `MysterySearch` (7 marks)

This is a **Linear Search** algorithm.

**Step 1 — Count operations per iteration:** Each loop iteration performs:
- 1 comparison `$i < n-1$`
- 1 array access `A[i]`
- 1 comparison `A[i] = key`
- Possibly 1 return statement.

So each iteration costs $O(1)$ basic operations.

**Step 2 — Number of iterations:** The loop runs $i = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ and stops either at first match or at $i = n$ (full scan).

**Step 3 — Best Case:** Key is at the **first position** $A[0]$. Loop runs exactly 1 iteration.
$$
T_{\text{best}}(n) = O(1)
$$

**Step 4 — Worst Case:** Key is at the **last position** $A[n-1]$ OR key is **not present** in the array. Loop runs $n$ iterations.
$$
T_{\text{worst}}(n) = O(n)
$$

**Step 5 — Average Case:** Assuming key is equally likely at any of the $n$ positions, the expected number of iterations is:
$$
T_{\text{avg}}(n) = \frac{1 + 2 + 3 + \cdots + n}{n} = \frac{n(n+1)/2}{n} = \frac{n+1}{2} = \Theta(n)
$$

**Step 6 — Final Big-O statement:** The asymptotic worst-case time complexity is:
$$
\boxed{T(n) = O(n)}
$$

**Conclusion:** `MysterySearch` is a linear search, useful for unsorted data, with $O(n)$ worst-case time. For sorted data, binary search would reduce this to $O(\log n)$.

*— 7 marks*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing ADT with Data Structure** — Deduct **2 marks** if the student uses these terms interchangeably. They are NOT the same.
> 2. **Writing "obvious" or "it is clear that"** in Big-O proofs — You MUST explicitly state witnesses $c$ and $n_0$. No proof = no marks.
> 3. **Confusing Big-O with Big-Theta** — Big-O is a *ceiling*, Big-Theta is a *tight sandwich*. Many students say "$O$" when they mean "$\Theta$."
> 4. **Forgetting to count pre-conditions** in ADT definitions — ADT specification is incomplete without pre-conditions, post-conditions, and error conditions.
> 5. **Mixing up best/worst/average** — Worst case gives the **upper bound** ($O$), best case gives the **lower bound** ($\Omega$). Average case requires probability distribution assumption.
> 6. **Writing the pipe symbol `|` in a markdown table** — Always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX, otherwise your table renders broken in the answer sheet.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Basic Concepts**

- ✅ **Data Structure** = Triplet $(D, R, O)$ — Data, Relationships, Operations.
- ✅ Two classification axes: **Linear vs Non-Linear** AND **Static vs Dynamic**.
- ✅ **Primitive types** (int, float, char, bool, pointer) are atomic; **Non-primitive** (array, stack, tree, graph) are derived.
- ✅ **ADT** = logical specification (WHAT). **Data Structure** = physical implementation (HOW).
- ✅ **Five canonical operations:** Traverse, Insert, Delete, Search, Sort.
- ✅ **Big-O** = upper bound; **Big-Omega** = lower bound; **Big-Theta** = tight bound.
- ✅ **Asymptotic dominance:** Drop lower-order terms and constants.
- ✅ **Growth hierarchy:** $O(1) \lt O(\log n) \lt O(n) \lt O(n \log n) \lt O(n^2) \lt O(2^n) \lt O(n!)$.
- ✅ **Time complexity** $T(n)$ is measured in number of basic operations, NOT seconds.
- ✅ **Best/Worst/Average** cases are mandatory for any algorithm analysis.
- ✅ **Linear search** = $O(n)$; **Binary search** = $O(\log n)$; **Mergesort/Quicksort** = $O(n \log n)$.
- ✅ Always prove Big-O statements by exhibiting explicit constants $c$ and $n_0$.
- ✅ **Properties to memorize:** Reflexivity, Transitivity, Symmetry, Sum Rule, Product Rule.
- ✅ In ADT specification, include: Data, Operations, Pre-conditions, Post-conditions, Error conditions.
- ✅ **Pipe symbol safety:** Use $\vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ in LaTeX to avoid breaking markdown tables.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Abstraction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST611 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Abstraction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Data Abstraction** is the process of defining a data structure in terms of its *logical (or mathematical) properties* and the *operations* that can be performed on it, while deliberately *hiding the internal implementation details* from the user. It is a programming and design principle that allows a programmer to focus on **what** a data object does rather than **how** it does it.

The KTU 2024 Scheme formally defines it through three inseparable pillars:

| Pillar | Formal Name | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Pillar 1 | **Data Object** | A mathematical object representing a collection of data values along with the relationships that exist among them. |
| Pillar 2 | **Abstract Data Type (ADT)** | A mathematical model for data types where a data type is defined by its *behavior* (semantics) from the point of view of a user, specifically in terms of possible values, possible operations, and the behavior of these operations. |
| Pillar 3 | **Encapsulation** | The mechanism that binds together the data and the operations that manipulate that data, and keeps both safe from external interference and misuse. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (High-Yield KTU Exam Point):**
> **Data Abstraction** = the *concept* of hiding implementation.
> **Encapsulation** = the *mechanism* (often using classes/access specifiers in C++/Java, or `struct` + opaque pointers in C) that enforces that hiding.
> **Data Type** = a category of data (e.g., `int`, `float`).
> **Abstract Data Type (ADT)** = a theoretical data type defined by behavior, independent of implementation.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Car Dashboard" Intuition

Imagine you are driving a **car**:

- You interact with the **steering wheel**, **accelerator**, **brake**, and **gear lever**.
- You do **NOT** need to understand how the internal combustion engine burns fuel, how the gearbox synchronizes cogs, or how hydraulic fluid transmits pressure to the brake pads.
- The **dashboard** (interface) abstracts away the **engine room** (implementation).

This is precisely **Data Abstraction**:
- The **driver** = the *user/programmer* using the ADT.
- The **dashboard controls** = the *public operations* (e.g., `push()`, `pop()`, `enqueue()`).
- The **engine room** = the *hidden internal representation* (e.g., an array, a linked list, a tree used internally).
- The **car's manual** = the *ADT specification* (contract of behavior).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 1 Expectation:**
> Students are expected to understand that an ADT is a **specification** (a *what*), while a **Data Structure** is an **implementation** (a *how*). The same ADT (e.g., Stack) can be implemented using *different* data structures (array-based stack, linked-list-based stack).

---

## 1.3 Why Data Abstraction Matters in KTU-Centric Engineering

In real-time systems (think of a pacemaker firmware, an ABS braking ECU, or an Airbus flight-control module), the software must be:

- **Modular** — teams can work on independent modules.
- **Maintainable** — swapping a linked-list internal storage for a hash-map should not break the calling code.
- **Verifiable** — formal contracts (pre-conditions/post-conditions) allow mathematical proof of correctness.

**Data Abstraction** delivers all three by enforcing a strict *interface-implementation separation*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Layered View of a Data Type

Every data type in the KTU 2024 syllabus can be examined through four distinct abstraction layers. Examiners love asking students to identify which layer a given statement belongs to.

| Layer # | Layer Name | Focus | Example (Stack ADT) |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| **L1** | **Mathematical (Logical) Layer** | What properties the data holds | Stack = LIFO ordered sequence |
| **L2** | **Abstract (Specification) Layer** | What operations are allowed and their effects | `Push(x)`, `Pop()`, `Top()`, `IsEmpty()` |
| **L3** | **Implementation (Representation) Layer** | How data is stored in memory | Array of fixed size `MAX`, or singly linked list of nodes |
| **L4** | **Physical (Hardware) Layer** | How memory cells are physically addressed | Contiguous bytes at addresses `0x4F00`–`0x4FA0` |

> [!TIP]
> **L1 + L2 = Data Abstraction.** **L3 + L4 = Implementation.**
> The boundary between L2 and L3 is the famous **abstraction barrier**. Crossing it violates the principle of data abstraction.

---

## 2.2 Components of an ADT Specification (KTU Board Format)

A complete ADT specification, as expected in KTU 14-mark answers, must contain **five mandatory elements**:

1. **Name** of the ADT (e.g., `Stack`, `Queue`, `List`).
2. **Data (Domain)** — what values the ADT can hold.
3. **Operations (Functions / Methods)** — the public interface.
4. **Pre-conditions** — conditions that must be true *before* an operation is invoked.
5. **Post-conditions** — conditions guaranteed to be true *after* the operation completes successfully.

### 2.2.1 Formal Pre/Post-condition Notation

For any operation `op(x)`:

$$
\text{Pre}(op) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{State}_{before}
$$

$$
\text{State}_{after} \;\Leftarrow\; \text{Post}(op)
$$

And the contract is:

$$
\text{Pre}(op) \;\land\; \text{op}(x) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Post}(op)
$$

> Where $\Rightarrow$ denotes logical implication. This is the **Hoare-logic** style of ADT specification referenced in advanced KTU textbooks.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Property Cheat Sheet — Data Abstraction

> [!NOTE]
> Since Data Abstraction is conceptual, the "formula sheet" is a properties & invariants table. Memorize the entire table for the 3-mark and 14-mark questions.

| # | Property / Rule | Formal Statement | Exam Weight |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|
| 1 | **Abstraction Principle** | $I = \langle D, O \rangle$ where $D$ = Data domain, $O$ = Set of operations | High |
| 2 | **Encapsulation Invariant** | Internal representation $\not\in$ public interface | High |
| 3 | **Information Hiding** | For any user $U$ and internal state $S$: $\text{User}(U) \cap \text{Internal}(S) = \emptyset$ | Medium |
| 4 | **ADT-DS Distinction** | $\text{ADT} \neq \text{Data Structure}$; $\text{ADT} \xrightarrow{\text{implements}} \text{Data Structure}$ | Very High |
| 5 | **LIFO Property (Stack ADT)** | $\text{Pop}()$ returns the element inserted most recently by $\text{Push}()$ | High |
| 6 | **FIFO Property (Queue ADT)** | $\text{Dequeue}()$ returns the element inserted longest ago by $\text{Enqueue}()$ | High |
| 7 | **Multiple Implementation Rule** | $\vert \text{Implementations of an ADT} \vert \;\geq\; 1$ (often $\geq 2$ in syllabus) | Medium |
| 8 | **Pre-condition Violation** | If $\neg \text{Pre}(op)$, behavior is *undefined* (caller's fault) | Medium |
| 9 | **Post-condition Guarantee** | If $\text{Pre}(op)$ holds, then $\text{Post}(op)$ *must* hold (implementer's contract) | Medium |
| 10 | **Modularity Metric** | $\text{Cohesion} \uparrow, \quad \text{Coupling} \downarrow$ | Low |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | ADT Used | Benefit |
|---|---|---|
| Embedded Systems (Automotive ECU) | Circular Buffer (Queue ADT) | Deterministic, bounded memory |
| Compiler Design | Symbol Table (Map/Dictionary ADT) | O(1) average lookups |
| Operating Systems | Process Stack (Stack ADT) | Function call management, context switching |
| Network Routers | Priority Queue (Heap ADT) | QoS packet scheduling |
| Database Engines | B-Tree (Ordered Map ADT) | Indexed disk access |
| Graphics / Game Engines | Scene Graph (Tree ADT) | Hierarchical rendering |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the pattern: **engineers choose the ADT first** (the logical contract), then **pick the data structure** that best satisfies performance constraints. This is the engineering workflow KTU 2024 expects you to articulate.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough: Designing a Complete ADT Specification

> The KTU 2024 Scheme frequently awards **7 marks** for a clean ADT specification. The following exhaustive walkthrough demonstrates the exact steps a top-scoring answer should follow.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example: ADT Specification of a *Stack of Integers*

### Step 1 — Identify the Real-World Problem
We need a container that supports **LIFO (Last-In, First-Out)** access, useful for function-call management, expression evaluation, and backtracking algorithms.

### Step 2 — Define the Data Domain $D$
The data domain is the set of all *valid states* the ADT can occupy. For a Stack of integers with capacity $N$:

$$
D = \left\{ \; s \in \mathbb{Z}^* \;\big\vert\; 0 \leq \vert s \vert \leq N \; \right\}
$$

Here $\mathbb{Z}^*$ denotes the set of all finite sequences of integers, and $\vert s \vert$ denotes the length (number of elements) in sequence $s$.

### Step 3 — Enumerate the Primitive Operations $O$
A minimal canonical interface (KTU 2024 expected answer):

$$
O = \left\{ \; \text{create}() \;,\; \text{push}(x) \;,\; \text{pop}() \;,\; \text{top}() \;,\; \text{isEmpty}() \;,\; \text{isFull}() \; \right\}
$$

### Step 4 — Specify Each Operation with Pre/Post-Conditions

For every operation, we express the contract formally.

**Operation 1: `create()`**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pre}(\text{create}) &::= \text{true} \\
\text{Post}(\text{create}) &::= \text{returns an empty stack } s \text{ such that } \vert s \vert = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Operation 2: `push(x)`**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pre}(\text{push}(x)) &::= \text{Stack is not full } (\vert s \vert < N) \;\land\; x \in \mathbb{Z} \\
\text{Post}(\text{push}(x)) &::= s' = s \circ \langle x \rangle \quad \text{(sequence concatenation, } x \text{ becomes new top)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Operation 3: `pop()`**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pre}(\text{pop}()) &::= \text{Stack is not empty } (\vert s \vert > 0) \\
\text{Post}(\text{pop}()) &::= \text{returns } s[\vert s \vert] \text{ and } s' = s[1 \ldots \vert s \vert - 1]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Operation 4: `top()`**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pre}(\text{top}()) &::= \text{Stack is not empty } (\vert s \vert > 0) \\
\text{Post}(\text{top}()) &::= \text{returns } s[\vert s \vert] \text{ and } s' = s \quad \text{(stack unchanged)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Operation 5: `isEmpty()`**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pre}(\text{isEmpty}()) &::= \text{true} \\
\text{Post}(\text{isEmpty}()) &::= \text{returns } (\vert s \vert = 0)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Operation 6: `isFull()`**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pre}(\text{isFull}()) &::= \text{true} \\
\text{Post}(\text{isFull}()) &::= \text{returns } (\vert s \vert = N)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Verify the Contract Logic
A top-grade KTU answer ends with a **correctness check**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Invariant } \Phi(s) &::= 0 \leq \vert s \vert \leq N \\
\text{Initially} &::= \Phi(s_0) \text{ holds because } \vert s_0 \vert = 0 \\
\text{Preservation under push} &::= \text{If } \vert s \vert < N, \text{ then } \vert s \circ \langle x \rangle \vert = \vert s \vert + 1 \leq N \\
\text{Preservation under pop} &::= \text{If } \vert s \vert > 0, \text{ then } \vert s' \vert = \vert s \vert - 1 \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, $\Phi$ is a **valid invariant** of the Stack ADT, satisfying both initialization and preservation — the two requirements of an inductive invariant proof.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing an ADT for a Given Problem

| # | Scenario | Correct ADT | Justification |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Browser "Back" button | **Stack** | LIFO; most recent URL popped first |
| 2 | CPU process scheduling (round-robin) | **Queue** | FIFO; oldest task served first |
| 3 | Recursive function calls | **Stack** | Call frames pushed/popped |
| 4 | Print spooler | **Queue** | First submitted, first printed |
| 5 | Undo/Redo in text editor | **Two Stacks** | Undo stack + Redo stack |
| 6 | Expression tree evaluation | **Stack** (operator/operand) | Shunting-yard algorithm |
| 7 | Hospital triage | **Priority Queue** | Severity, not arrival order |
| 8 | Dictionary lookup | **Map / Hash Table ADT** | Key-value, O(1) access |

---

## 3.3 Side-by-Side Comparison: ADT vs Data Structure (Board-Ready Table)

| Aspect | Abstract Data Type (ADT) | Data Structure |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Logical / Mathematical | Physical / Concrete |
| **Focus** | *What* operations are allowed | *How* operations are implemented |
| **Exists at** | Design phase | Implementation phase |
| **Language Independent?** | Yes | No (language specific) |
| **Example** | Stack specification | Array-based stack / Linked stack |
| **Visibility** | Public to the user | Hidden from the user |
| **Determined by** | Problem requirements | Performance / memory constraints |
| **Concrete?** | Abstract | Concrete |
| **Tested by** | Contract verification | Unit + Performance testing |
| **KTU Mark Allocation** | 7–10 marks in 14-mark Q | 4–7 marks in 14-mark Q |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Four-Layer Abstraction Hierarchy (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["LAYER 1 - MATHEMATICAL MODEL: LIFO Property, Sequences of Z"] --> L2["LAYER 2 - ADT SPECIFICATION: Push Pop Top IsEmpty IsFull"]
    L2 --> L3["LAYER 3 - IMPLEMENTATION CHOICE A: Array of size N with top index"]
    L2 --> L4["LAYER 3 - IMPLEMENTATION CHOICE B: Singly Linked List of Nodes"]
    L3 --> L5["LAYER 4 - PHYSICAL MEMORY: Contiguous bytes at base address"]
    L4 --> L6["LAYER 4 - PHYSICAL MEMORY: Scattered heap nodes with pointers"]
    L1 -.->|Abstraction Barrier| L2
    L2 -.->|Abstraction Barrier| L3
    L2 -.->|Abstraction Barrier| L4
    L3 -.->|Abstraction Barrier| L5
    L4 -.->|Abstraction Barrier| L6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The *dashed* lines represent the **abstraction barrier** — code on the upper layer **must not** reach across and access details of a lower layer. Violating this barrier is the most common design error flagged in KTU viva voce.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Data Abstraction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph USER_DOMAIN["USER DOMAIN - PROGRAMMER PERSPECTIVE"]
        A["Client Application Code"] --> B["ADT Interface Header File"]
    end
    subgraph ADT_SPEC["ADT SPECIFICATION LAYER"]
        B --> C["Public Operations Push Pop Top IsEmpty"]
        C --> D["Pre Conditions and Post Conditions Contract"]
    end
    subgraph IMPL_DOMAIN["IMPLEMENTATION DOMAIN - HIDDEN FROM USER"]
        D --> E["Internal Data Representation Array or Linked List"]
        D --> F["Private Helper Functions Resize Overflow Check"]
    end
    subgraph PHYS_DOMAIN["PHYSICAL MEMORY LAYER"]
        E --> G["Stack Memory or Heap Memory Allocation"]
        F --> G
    end
    USER_DOMAIN -.->|Boundary 1| ADT_SPEC
    ADT_SPEC -.->|Boundary 2 Encapsulation| IMPL_DOMAIN
    IMPL_DOMAIN -.->|Boundary 3| PHYS_DOMAIN
```

---

## 4.3 Concept Map: Data Abstraction and Its Sub-Concepts

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Data Abstraction))
    ADT
      Data Domain
      Operations
      Pre Conditions
      Post Conditions
    Encapsulation
      Public Interface
      Private State
      Access Specifiers
    Information Hiding
      Abstraction Barrier
      Modularity
    Design Principles
      Cohesion
      Coupling Reduction
      Reusability
    Common ADT Examples
      Stack
      Queue
      List
      Map
      Tree
```

---

## 4.4 Sequence Diagram: Validating a `push(x)` Call Against Its Contract

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Caller as Client Code
    participant API as ADT Interface
    participant Pre as Pre condition Check
    participant Op as push Operation
    participant Post as Post condition Check
    participant State as Internal State
    Caller->>API: invoke push x equals 42
    API->>Pre: is Stack not full
    Pre-->>API: TRUE
    API->>Op: execute push
    Op->>State: append 42 to internal storage
    State-->>Op: storage updated
    Op->>Post: verify new state
    Post-->>API: Post condition holds
    API-->>Caller: return success
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the term Abstract Data Type (ADT). Give one example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Abstract Data Type (ADT)** is a mathematical model for data types in which a data type is defined by its **behavior (semantics)** from the user's point of view — specifically in terms of **possible values, possible operations on those values, and the behavior of those operations** — *without* specifying how the data is stored in memory or how the operations are implemented.

**Components of an ADT:**
1. A set of **values** (data domain)
2. A set of **operations** defined on those values
3. A set of **axioms / contracts** (pre-conditions and post-conditions) describing semantics

**Example:** The **Stack** ADT, which supports `push()`, `pop()`, `top()`, and `isEmpty()`, with the LIFO property.

> *(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the three components, 1 mark for example.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between data abstraction and data encapsulation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Data Abstraction | Data Encapsulation |
|---|---|---|
| **Concept** | Hiding *complexity* by exposing only essential features | Hiding *data* by bundling it with methods that operate on it |
| **Mechanism** | ADT specification (interface) | Language-level access control (e.g., `public`/`private`) |
| **Focus** | *What* an object does | *How* the data is protected |
| **Achieved via** | Abstract classes / interfaces / header files | Classes / structures / modules |
| **Example** | Showing only `push()`/`pop()` to the user | Hiding the internal `array[]` and `top` index variable |

> *(Award 1 mark per correct row × 3 rows.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module 1 Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Data Abstraction** in detail. Discuss its significance in software engineering with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the complete **ADT specification** of a *Stack of Characters* with the operations `create()`, `push(ch)`, `pop()`, `peek()`, `isEmpty()`, and `isSize()`. State the pre-condition and post-condition for every operation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**
Data Abstraction is the methodology of describing data structures in terms of their *logical/behavioral properties* and the *set of operations* that may be performed on them, while concealing the internal storage representation and implementation algorithms from the outside world.

**Step 2 — Components (2 marks):**
The three primary components are:
- **Data Objects:** Mathematical entities that hold values and relationships.
- **Operations:** Functions that act on data objects.
- **Encapsulation & Information Hiding:** Mechanisms that prevent unauthorized access to internal state.

**Step 3 — Significance (2 marks):**
1. **Modularity:** Independent modules can be developed and tested.
2. **Reusability:** A well-specified ADT (e.g., Stack) can be reused across projects.
3. **Maintainability:** Internal implementation can be changed (array → linked list) without altering client code.
4. **Correctness:** Pre/post-conditions enable formal verification.

**Step 4 — Example (1 mark):**
Consider the **List ADT** — a user can call `insert()`, `delete()`, `find()` without knowing whether the list is internally an array or a linked list.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**ADT: `StackOfChar`**

**Data Domain:**

$$
D = \left\{ s \in \Sigma^* \;\big\vert\; 0 \leq \vert s \vert \leq N, \; \Sigma = \text{set of ASCII characters} \right\}
$$

**Operations with Pre/Post-Conditions:**

1. `create()`

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pre}(\text{create}) &::= \text{true} \\
\text{Post}(\text{create}) &::= \text{returns } s \text{ with } \vert s \vert = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

2. `push(ch)`

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pre}(\text{push}(ch)) &::= \vert s \vert < N \;\land\; ch \in \Sigma \\
\text{Post}(\text{push}(ch)) &::= s' = s \circ \langle ch \rangle
\end{aligned}
$$

3. `pop()`

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pre}(\text{pop}()) &::= \vert s \vert > 0 \\
\text{Post}(\text{pop}()) &::= \text{returns } s[\vert s \vert] \text{ and } s' = s[1 \ldots \vert s \vert - 1]
\end{aligned}
$$

4. `peek()`

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pre}(\text{peek}()) &::= \vert s \vert > 0 \\
\text{Post}(\text{peek}()) &::= \text{returns } s[\vert s \vert] \text{ and } s' = s
\end{aligned}
$$

5. `isEmpty()`

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pre}(\text{isEmpty}()) &::= \text{true} \\
\text{Post}(\text{isEmpty}()) &::= \text{returns } (\vert s \vert = 0)
\end{aligned}
$$

6. `isSize()`

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pre}(\text{isSize}()) &::= \text{true} \\
\text{Post}(\text{isSize}()) &::= \text{returns } \vert s \vert \text{ and } s' = s
\end{aligned}
$$

> *(Award 2 marks for data domain + name, 4 marks for correct pre/post-conditions of all 6 operations, 1 mark for proper notation.)*

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **four abstraction levels** of a data type. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Abstract Data Types (ADTs)** with **Data Structures**. Illustrate with the example of a *Queue*. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**The Four Abstraction Levels:**

| Level | Name | Description | Stack Example |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| **L1** | Mathematical Layer | Pure logical properties, sequence notation | $s = \langle a, b, c \rangle$ |
| **L2** | Logical/Abstract Layer | Operations and their semantics | `push(x)`, `pop()`, `top()` |
| **L3** | Implementation Layer | Concrete storage + algorithms | `int arr[N]; int top = -1;` |
| **L4** | Physical Layer | Memory addresses and cells | `0x4F00`, `0x4F04`, … |

**Diagram (3 marks):**

```
   L1  |  Mathematical Model
        v
   L2  |  ADT Specification (abstract operations)
        v
   L3  |  Data Structure (array / linked list)
        v
   L4  |  Physical Memory (RAM cells)
```

Each downward arrow represents *implementation*, and each upward arrow represents *abstraction*.

> *(Award 2 marks for naming all 4 levels, 2 marks for examples, 3 marks for diagram.)*

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Aspect | ADT (e.g., Queue) | Data Structure (e.g., Circular Array Queue) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | A queue is a FIFO-ordered sequence of elements | An array `Q[MAX]` with `front` and `rear` indices, modular arithmetic |
| **Operations** | `enqueue(x)`, `dequeue()`, `front()`, `isEmpty()` | Same names, but with index manipulation |
| **Implementation** | Hidden | Concrete `int Q[MAX];` and pointer arithmetic |
| **Behavior** | FIFO guarantee | FIFO guarantee *only if* `rear` wraps correctly |
| **Performance** | Not specified | `enqueue` = O(1), `dequeue` = O(1) |
| **Multiple Implementations** | Yes (also: linked queue, deque) | This *specific* one is fixed |

**Illustration (2 marks):**
For the **Queue ADT**, two valid implementations exist:
- **Circular Array-based Queue** — uses `front`, `rear`, and `(rear + 1) \bmod N`.
- **Linked-List-based Queue** — uses a `head` and `tail` pointer.

Both honor the FIFO contract of the Queue ADT but differ in memory and time trade-offs.

> *(Award 5 marks for the comparison table, 2 marks for the two implementations.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here!):**
> 1. **Confusing ADT with Data Structure** — Examiners deduct up to 2 marks if you call a Stack a "data structure" without clarifying it is first an *ADT* and then implemented via a *data structure*.
> 2. **Skipping pre/post-conditions** — A 7-mark ADT specification question that omits pre/post-conditions is capped at **3–4 marks**, even if all operations are listed.
> 3. **Drawing the abstraction barrier incorrectly** — In 14-mark questions, drawing the wrong boundary (e.g., between L1 and L2) costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Using implementation terms in the ADT layer** — Saying "Stack uses an array" inside an ADT specification is an automatic **−2 mark** penalty. The ADT layer is *language- and implementation-agnostic*.
> 5. **Forgetting the invariant proof** — When asked to "verify" or "validate" an ADT, failing to show initialization + preservation of the invariant costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this checklist as your last-minute revision sheet (30 minutes before the exam).**

- [ ] **Data Abstraction** = *hiding complexity by exposing only essential behavior* (the *what*, not the *how*).
- [ ] **ADT** = a *mathematical specification* consisting of **Data Domain + Operations + Pre/Post-conditions**.
- [ ] **Encapsulation** is the *mechanism*; **Abstraction** is the *principle*.
- [ ] **Data Structure** is the *concrete implementation*; **ADT** is the *logical contract*.
- [ ] The **Abstraction Barrier** lies between L2 (ADT) and L3 (Implementation) — never cross it from the user side.
- [ ] Four Layers: **Mathematical → Abstract → Implementation → Physical** (top-down design, bottom-up understanding).
- [ ] A single ADT can have **multiple valid implementations** (Stack → array or linked list; Queue → circular array or linked list).
- [ ] **Pre-condition** = the caller's responsibility; **Post-condition** = the implementer's guarantee.
- [ ] **Cohesion ↑, Coupling ↓** — the golden rule of modular ADT design.
- [ ] **Stack ADT** = LIFO; **Queue ADT** = FIFO — never mix them up in scenario-based questions.
- [ ] **Invariant** $\Phi(s)$ must satisfy *Initialization* ($s_0$ satisfies $\Phi$) and *Preservation* (every operation preserves $\Phi$).
- [ ] Standard ADT interface (memorize): `create`, `push`/`enqueue`/`insert`, `pop`/`dequeue`/`delete`, `top`/`front`/`peek`, `isEmpty`, `isFull`/`isSize`.
- [ ] Common *one-liner definition* to memorize: *"An ADT is a data type defined by its behavior rather than its implementation."*
- [ ] KTU 2024 frequent exam traps: ADT ≠ Data Structure; Abstract = public-facing; Encapsulation = private-state; **Stack ≠ Array** (Stack is the ADT, Array can be its implementation).
- [ ] Real-world examples to quote in 14-mark answers: Browser back button (Stack), Print spooler (Queue), Compiler symbol table (Map), OS process scheduling (Priority Queue).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Analysis: Time and Space Complexity, Asymptotic Notations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST611 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Performance Analysis: Time and Space Complexity, Asymptotic Notations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Performance Analysis — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Analysis** is the systematic process of evaluating an algorithm in terms of the **computational resources** it consumes — primarily **Time** (CPU cycles) and **Space** (memory) — as a function of the **input size $n$**.

In the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education framework, this topic directly maps to **Course Outcome CO1** of *OECST611 (Data Structures)*, which expects students to *"analyse the time and space complexity of algorithms using asymptotic notations."*

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Commute Problem 🛣️**
>
> Imagine you must reach KTU Campus from your home. **Google Maps** offers you *three routes*:
>
> - **Route 1 (Algorithm A):** Longest path through city traffic — slow but uses less fuel.
> - **Route 2 (Algorithm B):** Shortest highway route — fast but toll costs money (extra memory).
> - **Route 3 (Algorithm C):** Balanced mid-distance road — moderate time and fuel.
>
> **Performance Analysis** is the act of measuring these three routes *objectively* (time, fuel, toll) so you can pick the *best route* (algorithm) for the *longest distance* (large input $n$).
>
> For a *small* trip (small $n$), Route 1 might work fine. But for a *cross-country* trip (large $n$), Route 2 is the *asymptotically* optimal choice.

### 1.3 Why Not Just Time the Code? (The Need for Asymptotic Analysis)

Empirical timing is **unreliable** because:

- Hardware varies (Intel i3 vs Apple M2).
- Compiler optimizations differ.
- Input data distributions differ.
- Constant factors dominate for small $n$.

Therefore, we use **asymptotic analysis** — a *mathematical*, *machine-independent*, and *input-size-driven* model.

### 1.4 Core Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Time Complexity $T(n)$** — The number of elementary operations (additions, comparisons, assignments) an algorithm performs as a function of input size $n$.
>
> **Space Complexity $S(n)$** — The total memory an algorithm requires, given by:
>
> $$S(n) = \text{Fixed Part} + \text{Variable Part}$$
>
> where **Fixed Part** = instruction space, constants, simple variables; **Variable Part** = dynamically allocated memory (recursion stack, arrays whose size depends on $n$).

**Standard Metrics used by KTU Examiners:**

- **$\mathbf{n}$** → input size (length of array, number of nodes, etc.)
- **$\mathbf{T(n)}$** → exact number of primitive operations
- **$\mathbf{S(n)}$** → bytes (or word) count
- **$\mathbf{c_i}$** → cost of the $i^{\text{th}}$ operation
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The RAM Model of Computation

To make analysis rigorous, KTU follows the **Random Access Machine (RAM)** model with these assumptions:

1. Each simple operation ($+, -, *, /, =, ==, <$) takes **1 unit of time**.
2. Loops and function calls are *not* simple operations.
3. Memory access is **uniform** (no cache effects).
4. Each memory cell holds one *word* (typically **4 or 8 bytes**).

### 2.2 The Three Asymptotic Notations (KTU High-Yield Core)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a given function $T(n)$, the three notations bound its growth from different sides. Let $g(n)$ be a known reference function.

#### 2.2.1 **Big-O Notation $O(g(n))$** — *Upper Bound* (Worst-Case Ceiling)

$$T(n) = O(g(n)) \iff \exists\, c > 0,\ n_0 > 0 \text{ such that } 0 \le T(n) \le c \cdot g(n) \;\; \forall\, n \ge n_0$$

**Intuition:** $T(n)$ grows **no faster than** $g(n)$ for sufficiently large $n$.

#### 2.2.2 **Big-Omega Notation $\Omega(g(n))$** — *Lower Bound* (Best-Case Floor)

$$T(n) = \Omega(g(n)) \iff \exists\, c > 0,\ n_0 > 0 \text{ such that } 0 \le c \cdot g(n) \le T(n) \;\; \forall\, n \ge n_0$$

**Intuition:** $T(n)$ grows **at least as fast as** $g(n)$ for sufficiently large $n$.

#### 2.2.3 **Big-Theta Notation $\Theta(g(n))$** — *Tight Bound* (Average/Exact Growth)

$$T(n) = \Theta(g(n)) \iff \exists\, c_1, c_2 > 0,\ n_0 > 0 \text{ such that } c_1 \cdot g(n) \le T(n) \le c_2 \cdot g(n) \;\; \forall\, n \ge n_0$$

**Intuition:** $T(n)$ grows **at the same rate as** $g(n)$, sandwiched between two constants.

#### 2.2.4 Supporting Notations (Frequently tested in Part A)

- **Little-o $o(g(n))$** → $T(n)$ grows *strictly* slower than $g(n)$. Used for comparing *distinct* functions.
- **Little-omega $\omega(g(n))$** → $T(n)$ grows *strictly* faster than $g(n)$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **single most important reference** for KTU exams. Memorize the growth rate ordering and the operation count for each.

| Complexity Class | Name | Operation Count $T(n)$ | Sample Code Pattern | Verdict for $n = 10^6$ |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|:---:|
| $O(1)$ | Constant | $1$ | `arr[i]`, hash lookup | ⚡ Instant |
| $O(\log n)$ | Logarithmic | $\log_2 n$ | Binary search, balanced BST | ✅ Excellent |
| $O(n)$ | Linear | $n$ | Single loop, linear search | ✅ Good |
| $O(n \log n)$ | Linearithmic | $n \log_2 n$ | Merge sort, heap sort | ✅ Good |
| $O(n^2)$ | Quadratic | $n^2$ | Nested loops (Bubble, Selection) | ⚠️ Slow |
| $O(n^3)$ | Cubic | $n^3$ | Triple nested loops (matrix mul) | ❌ Very slow |
| $O(2^n)$ | Exponential | $2^n$ | Naive Fibonacci, subset enumeration | ❌ Impractical |
| $O(n!)$ | Factorial | $n!$ | Permutations (TSP brute force) | 💀 Impossible |

**Universal Ordering (ascending slowness):**
$$O(1) \prec O(\log n) \prec O(n) \prec O(n \log n) \prec O(n^2) \prec O(n^3) \prec O(2^n) \prec O(n!)$$

### 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters

- **Compiler Design:** Asymptotic bounds guide loop unrolling, inlining, and instruction scheduling.
- **Database Systems:** B-Tree ($O(\log n)$) vs Linear Scan ($O(n)$) determines query performance at scale.
- **Operating Systems:** Process scheduling (priority queues) relies on heap operations with $O(\log n)$ bounds.
- **Networks:** Dijkstra's algorithm $O(E \log V)$ vs Bellman-Ford $O(VE)$ for shortest path.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Master Method: Deriving $T(n)$ From Code

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **five rules below** are how KTU examiners construct complexity questions. Every code snippet reduces to one of these.

#### **Rule 1 — Sequential Statements (Addition Rule)**

$$T(n) = T_1(n) + T_2(n) + \ldots + T_k(n) \implies T(n) = O(\max(T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_k))$$

**Example (C code):**
```c
int sum = 0;            // O(1)
for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) sum += arr[i];   // O(n)
printf("%d", sum);      // O(1)
```
$$T(n) = 1 + n + 1 = n + 2 \implies T(n) = O(n)$$

#### **Rule 2 — Single Loop (Multiplication Rule)**

`for (i = 1 to n)` performing $f(n)$ work inside → $n \cdot f(n)$.

#### **Rule 3 — Nested Loops (Product of Iterations)**

`for i (1→n) for j (1→n)` → $n \times n = n^2$.

#### **Rule 4 — Logarithmic Loops**

`for (i = 1; i < n; i *= 2)` → $\log_2 n$ iterations.

**Derivation (step-by-step):**
Let $k$ be the number of iterations. The loop ends when $i \ge n$, with $i$ doubling each time:
$$i = 1, 2, 4, 8, \ldots, 2^k \ge n \implies 2^k \ge n \implies k \ge \log_2 n$$
$$\therefore T(n) = O(\log n)$$

#### **Rule 5 — Recursive Calls (Master Theorem / Recurrence Tree)**

For $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$:
- If $f(n) = O(n^{\log_b a - \epsilon})$ → $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$
- If $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$ → $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log n)$
- If $f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_b a + \epsilon})$ → $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Examples

#### **Example 1: Prove $5n^2 + 3n + 10 = O(n^2)$**

We need $c > 0$ and $n_0 > 0$ such that $5n^2 + 3n + 10 \le c \cdot n^2$ for all $n \ge n_0$.

$$\begin{aligned}
5n^2 + 3n + 10 &\le 5n^2 + 3n^2 + 10n^2 \quad \text{(since } n \ge 1 \Rightarrow n \le n^2 \text{ and } 1 \le n^2\text{)} \\
&= 18n^2
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore, choosing $c = 18$ and $n_0 = 1$:
$$5n^2 + 3n + 10 \le 18n^2 \quad \forall\, n \ge 1 \quad \blacksquare$$

#### **Example 2: Prove $n^2 = O(n^3)$ but $n^3 \neq O(n^2)$**

**Part A:** $n^2 \le c \cdot n^3 \iff 1 \le c \cdot n$, which holds for $c = 1, n_0 = 1$. ✓

**Part B (by contradiction):** Suppose $n^3 = O(n^2)$. Then $\exists\, c, n_0$ such that:
$$n^3 \le c \cdot n^2 \implies n \le c \quad \forall\, n \ge n_0$$

But $n$ can be arbitrarily large, so $n \le c$ is violated. Contradiction. ✗

#### **Example 3: Time Complexity of Nested Loop with Inner Log Loop**

```c
for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++)           // outer: n times
    for (int j = 1; j <= i; j *= 2)    // inner: log i times
        count++;
```

**Step 1:** Inner loop iterates $\log_2 i$ times for each $i$.
**Step 2:** Total cost:
$$\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log_2 i \\
&= \log_2(1 \cdot 2 \cdot 3 \cdots n) \\
&= \log_2(n!) \approx n \log_2 n \quad \text{(by Stirling's approximation)}
\end{aligned}$$
$$\therefore T(n) = O(n \log n)$$

#### **Example 4: Space Complexity of Recursive Fibonacci**

```c
int fib(int n) {
    if (n <= 1) return n;
    return fib(n-1) + fib(n-2);
}
```

- **Time:** $T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + O(1) \implies T(n) = O(2^n)$.
- **Space:** Maximum recursion depth = $n$ (the deeper branch `fib(n-1)`). Each stack frame is $O(1)$ → $S(n) = O(n)$ (call stack), even though there are $2^n$ calls.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classic KTU trap:** $T(n) = 2^n$ but $S(n) = n$. Students often wrongly claim exponential space.

#### **Example 5: Best, Worst, Average Case — Linear Search**

```c
int linearSearch(int arr[], int n, int key) {
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
        if (arr[i] == key) return i;
    return -1;
}
```

| Case | Condition | Comparisons | Complexity |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **Best** | Key at index 0 | $1$ | $\Omega(1)$ |
| **Worst** | Key absent or at end | $n$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Average** | Key at random position | $\frac{n+1}{2}$ | $\Theta(n)$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Growth-Rate Comparison Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    start([Input size n grows]) --> q1{Loop type?}
    q1 -->|No loop| const[O of 1: Constant]
    q1 -->|Loop halves i| log[O of log n: Logarithmic]
    q1 -->|Single loop n| lin[O of n: Linear]
    q1 -->|Loop with n log n work| linlog[O of n log n]
    q1 -->|Two nested loops| quad[O of n squared: Quadratic]
    q1 -->|Three nested loops| cube[O of n cubed]
    q1 -->|Recursive doubling| exp[O of 2 to n: Exponential]
    q1 -->|Permutation recursion| fact[O of n factorial]
    const --> verdict1([Fastest: Instant])
    log --> verdict2([Excellent for huge n])
    lin --> verdict3([Acceptable])
    linlog --> verdict4([Good for sorting])
    quad --> verdict5([Slow beyond n equals 10 to power 4])
    cube --> verdict6([Impractical beyond n equals 500])
    exp --> verdict7([Dead at n equals 40])
    fact --> verdict8([Dead at n equals 12])
    style start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style verdict1 fill:#90EE90
    style verdict8 fill:#FF6347
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow: Choosing the Right Asymptotic Notation

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q[Given a function T of n] --> Q1{Is it an upper bound question?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| O1[Use Big O]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is it a lower bound question?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Omega1[Use Big Omega]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is it a tight exact bound?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Theta1[Use Big Theta]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Strict inequality needed?}
    Q4 -->|Upper strict| littleo1[Use little o]
    Q4 -->|Lower strict| littleom1[Use little omega]
    style O1 fill:#4A90E2,color:#fff
    style Omega1 fill:#E27D4A,color:#fff
    style Theta1 fill:#50C878,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Algorithm Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw C Code Snippet] --> B[Step 1: Identify Input Size n]
    B --> C[Step 2: Count Primitive Operations per Statement]
    C --> D[Step 3: Sum Costs Across Loops]
    D --> E[Step 4: Apply Asymptotic Simplification]
    E --> F[Step 5: Drop Lower Order Terms and Constants]
    F --> G[Final Complexity Class e.g. O of n log n]
    style A fill:#FFE4B5
    style G fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px
```

### 4.4 Visual Comparison: Big-O, Big-Ω, Big-Θ

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph BigO [Big O — Upper Bound]
        O1[growth line T of n] --- O2[ceiling c times g of n]
        O2 --- O3[region where T stays below]
    end
    subgraph BigOmega [Big Omega — Lower Bound]
        W1[floor c times g of n] --- W2[growth line T of n]
    end
    subgraph BigTheta [Big Theta — Tight Sandwich]
        TH1[lower c1 times g] --- TH2[T of n trapped between] --- TH3[upper c2 times g]
    end
    style BigO fill:#E0F3FF
    style BigOmega fill:#FFE0E0
    style BigTheta fill:#E0FFE0
```

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
*Define asymptotic notation. Explain Big-O notation with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Asymptotic notations are mathematical tools used to express the **running time** (or space) of an algorithm in terms of input size $n$ as $n$ approaches infinity.

**Big-O Notation** $O(g(n))$ provides an **upper bound** on the growth of $T(n)$.

$$T(n) = O(g(n)) \iff \exists\, c > 0,\, n_0 > 0 \text{ s.t. } T(n) \le c \cdot g(n) \;\; \forall\, n \ge n_0$$

**Example:** For $T(n) = 3n^2 + 5n + 10$, we have $T(n) = O(n^2)$ because the dominant term $3n^2$ governs growth and constants can be absorbed. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
*Distinguish between best case, worst case, and average case complexity with an example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Best Case ($\Omega$):** Minimum operations on the *most favourable* input. *Example:* Linear search finding the element at index 0 → $\Omega(1)$.
- **Worst Case ($O$):** Maximum operations on the *least favourable* input. *Example:* Linear search with element absent or at last position → $O(n)$.
- **Average Case ($\Theta$):** Expected operations over *all possible* inputs, assuming uniform distribution. *Example:* Linear search → $\Theta\!\left(\frac{n+1}{2}\right) = \Theta(n)$. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Module Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain Big-O, Big-Omega, and Big-Theta asymptotic notations. Prove that $6n^3 + 4n^2 + 2 = O(n^3)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the time complexity of the following code segment and justify with a step-by-step derivation: **(7 marks)**

```c
int mystery(int n) {
    int i, j, k, count = 0;
    for (i = n/2; i <= n; i++)
        for (j = 1; j <= n; j = j * 2)
            for (k = 1; k <= n; k = k * 2)
                count++;
    return count;
}
```

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Definition — Big-O** (upper bound): $\exists\, c, n_0 > 0$ such that $0 \le T(n) \le c \cdot g(n)$ for all $n \ge n_0$. **(1 mark)**

**Definition — Big-Ω** (lower bound): $\exists\, c, n_0 > 0$ such that $0 \le c \cdot g(n) \le T(n)$ for all $n \ge n_0$. **(1 mark)**

**Definition — Big-Θ** (tight bound): $\exists\, c_1, c_2, n_0 > 0$ such that $c_1 \cdot g(n) \le T(n) \le c_2 \cdot g(n)$ for all $n \ge n_0$. **(1 mark)**

**Visual explanation:** Big-O is the *ceiling*, Big-Ω is the *floor*, Big-Θ is the *sandwich*. **(1 mark)**

**Proof that $6n^3 + 4n^2 + 2 = O(n^3)$:** We seek $c$ and $n_0$ such that $6n^3 + 4n^2 + 2 \le c \cdot n^3$.

For $n \ge 1$: $n^2 \le n^3$ and $1 \le n^3$, so:
$$\begin{aligned}
6n^3 + 4n^2 + 2 &\le 6n^3 + 4n^3 + 2n^3 \\
&= 12n^3
\end{aligned}$$

Choosing $c = 12$ and $n_0 = 1$ satisfies the definition. Hence $6n^3 + 4n^2 + 2 = O(n^3)$. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Loop Analysis:**

- **Outer loop:** `i = n/2` to `n` → executes $n - n/2 + 1 \approx n/2$ times. **(1 mark)**
- **Middle loop:** `j` doubles from 1 → executes $\log_2 n$ times. **(1 mark)**
- **Inner loop:** `k` doubles from 1 → executes $\log_2 n$ times. **(1 mark)**

**Total work:**
$$\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \sum_{i=n/2}^{n} \sum_{j \text{ iter}} \sum_{k \text{ iter}} 1 \\
&= \left(\frac{n}{2}\right) \cdot (\log_2 n) \cdot (\log_2 n) \\
&= \frac{n}{2} \cdot (\log_2 n)^2
\end{aligned}$$

Dropping the constant $\frac{1}{2}$: **(2 marks)**
$$\therefore T(n) = O\!\left(n (\log n)^2\right)$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** $T(n) = O(n \log^2 n)$, which is asymptotically slower than $O(n \log n)$ but faster than $O(n^2)$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Define space complexity. Differentiate between fixed and variable parts of space with an example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Analyze the time complexity of the following recursive function using a recurrence relation and solve using the recursion tree method: **(7 marks)**

```c
int sum(int arr[], int n) {
    if (n == 0) return 0;
    return arr[n-1] + sum(arr, n-1);
}
```

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Definition:** Space complexity $S(n)$ is the total memory an algorithm requires as a function of input size $n$. **(1 mark)**

$$S(n) = \underbrace{C}_{\text{Fixed Part}} + \underbrace{S_{\text{instance}}(n)}_{\text{Variable Part}}$$

**Fixed Part $C$:** Independent of instance characteristics. Includes:
- Instruction space (compiled code)
- Space for simple variables, constants
- Space for return addresses, structured bindings **(2 marks)**

**Variable Part $S_{\text{instance}}(n)$:** Depends on instance. Includes:
- Dynamically allocated memory (`malloc`, `new`)
- Recursion stack space
- Arrays whose size depends on $n$ **(2 marks)**

**Example:** For `int arr[100]`, $S = \text{code size} + 100 \times 4 \text{ bytes} = C + 400$. The first term is fixed; the second is variable if `100` were replaced by $n$. **(2 marks)**

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Recurrence Relation:**

Let $T(n)$ be the time to sum an array of size $n$. The function does:
- One comparison `$n == 0$` → $O(1)$
- One addition `arr[n-1] + ...` → $O(1)$
- One recursive call on $n-1$ → $T(n-1)$

$$T(n) = T(n-1) + c, \quad T(0) = d$$

**Recursion Tree Expansion:** **(3 marks)**

$$T(n) = c + T(n-1) = c + c + T(n-2) = 2c + T(n-2) = \ldots = nc + T(0) = nc + d$$

**Solving with substitution method:** **(2 marks)**

Unrolling $k$ times: $T(n) = kc + T(n-k)$. Stop when $n-k = 0$, i.e., $k = n$.

$$T(n) = nc + d = O(n)$$

**Stack space (bonus):** $S(n) = O(n)$ due to recursion depth $n$. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Verified from KTU Valuation Keys):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Big-O with Worst Case always:** Big-O is just an *upper bound*, not synonymous with worst case. A best-case algorithm can also be expressed in Big-O.
> 2. **Forgetting constants vs asymptotics:** Students write "$T(n) = 2n$ is $O(n^2)$" — technically true but **mark-losing** because the tight bound is $O(n)$. Always state the **tightest** bound.
> 3. **Loop analysis errors:** `for (i = 1; i < n; i *= 2)` is $\log_2(n-1)$, not $\log_2 n$. Use the **Master Rule** strictly.
> 4. **Recursion ≠ Exponential Time:** Recursive Fibonacci is $O(2^n)$ *time* but $O(n)$ *space*. Don't mix up.
> 5. **Missing the base case** in Master Theorem applications → full marks lost.
> 6. **Forgetting to drop constants:** Writing $T(n) = 3n^2 + 5n$ as $O(3n^2)$ instead of $O(n^2)$ is considered imprecise by KTU examiners.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Time Complexity** counts elementary operations; **Space Complexity** counts memory cells.
- ✅ **Three Pillars:** $O$ (upper), $\Omega$ (lower), $\Theta$ (tight).
- ✅ **Loop Iteration Counts:** `i++` in `for` → $n$; `i *= 2` → $\log n$; `i *= 3` → $\log_3 n$.
- ✅ **Nested Loops Multiply:** two nested `for` loops each running $n$ times → $n^2$.
- ✅ **Sequential Blocks Add:** take the **maximum** of the two block complexities.
- ✅ **Recursion:** Always set up the recurrence $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ and apply Master Theorem.
- ✅ **Best/Average/Worst:** $\Omega / \Theta / O$ respectively — but they are *not* the same; they are analysis tools.
- ✅ **Drop Constants & Lower-Order Terms:** $3n^2 + 100n + 7 \to O(n^2)$.
- ✅ **Common Growth Hierarchy (ascending):** $1 < \log n < n < n \log n < n^2 < n^3 < 2^n < n!$.
- ✅ **Stirling's Approximation:** $n! \approx \sqrt{2\pi n}\,(n/e)^n$ — useful for factorial-time algorithms.
- ✅ **Space of Recursion = Maximum Call Stack Depth**, not total calls.
